text: ESP32-Based Portable Thermal Monitoring & Imaging System Yatin – SE22UECE007 Anshul – SE22UECM013 Siddharth – SE22UECM016 Introduction: ESP32-Based Portable Thermal Monitoring & Imaging System Thermal imaging enables non-contact temperature monitoring Widely used in healthcare, security, and industrial inspection Commercial thermal cameras are expensive and limited in accessibility Low-cost and portable thermal imaging solutions are still scarce Objectives: System Overview: Thermal data is captured from the surrounding environment An infrared sensor detects temperature variations ESP32 processes the data in real time A TFT display visualizes the output as a heatmap Hardware Components Used: 1. ESP32 (Bharat Pi) The ESP32 acts as the main controller, processing thermal sensor data and driving the TFT display for real-time visualization. 2. AMG8833 Infrared Thermal Sensor The AMG8833 measures non-contact temperature data in an 8×8 grid and provides real-time thermal readings to the ESP32. 3. 1.8-inch TFT Display The TFT display visualizes processed thermal data as a colour-based heatmap for easy interpretation. ESP32 (Bharat Pi): Working Principle: The thermal sensor measures temperature without physical contact ESP32 reads sensor data using the I²C protocol Temperature values are processed and scaled A color-based heatmap is generated and displayed Software Implementation: Required libraries are included to handle I²C communication, SPI communication, graphics rendering, and thermal sensor interfacing Pin definitions are set to interface the ESP32 with the TFT display and thermal sensor The ESP32 initializes the TFT display and AMG8833 sensor during the setup phase Thermal pixel data is continuously read from the AMG8833 sensor Minimum and maximum temperature values are dynamically calculated for each frame Bilinear interpolation is applied to smooth the low-resolution thermal data Temperature values are mapped to a color gradient ranging from blue (cool) to red (hot) The processed thermal image is rendered in real time on the TFT display Temperature Mapping & Visualization: Each pixel represents an individual temperature value Cooler regions appear in blue shades Warmer regions appear in red shades Gradual colour transitions indicate temperature differences Results & Observations: Real-time thermal visualization achieved Clear identification of heat sources Smooth and stable display output Portable and low-cost design validated Applications & Limitations: Applications Temperature monitoring Human presence detection Educational demonstrations Limitations Low spatial resolution due to 8 × 8 sensor Limited accuracy compared to commercial systems Conclusion & Future Scope: A compact thermal monitoring system was successfully developed Demonstrates effective use of embedded systems Affordable alternative to commercial thermal cameras Future improvements include

Temperature monitoring Human presence detection Educational demonstrations Limitations Low spatial resolution due to 8 × 8 sensor Limited accuracy compared to commercial systems Conclusion & Future Scope: A compact thermal monitoring system was successfully developed Demonstrates effective use of embedded systems Affordable alternative to commercial thermal cameras Future improvements include higher-resolution sensors and wireless visualization
